Catalog Description

Systems Engineering Methods and Frameworks. (3-0). Credit 3. Concepts,

methodology, methods and tools for discovery, definition, analysis, design, creation,

and sustainment of systems involving information, physical, and human elements;

architecture modeling methods include IDEF/UPDM; systems engineering

frameworks include DoDAF/MoDAF, and Zachman; analysis tools include executable

architectures to assess consistency, interoperability and performance.

Prerequisites: MATH 304 or approval of instructor.

Additional Course Details

course details

From the instructor

This course is designed to provide the students with an understanding of the

concepts, methodology, methods and tools of systems discovery, definition,

analysis, design, creation, and sustainment. The main focus of the course is to

develop the students’ critical thinking skills to 1) define and analyze the as-is

system, 2) develop and communicate the to-be system requirements, 3)

transition these requirements into the design specification of a new or modified

system, and 4) evaluate the value, correctness, interoperability, and performance

of a system design. ISEN 640 is highly recommended as a prerequisite for this

course

Course Prerequisites

Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  MATH 304 or approval of instructor.

Course Learning Outcomes

The student will learn the component methods for systems architecting including:

function modeling, process modeling, information and data modeling, ontology

modeling and representation, software and system behavioral and structural

design, systems dynamics modeling. The student will also learn the standard

frameworks for structuring the systems development process and documenting the

resulting artifacts. Finally the student will learn the core techniques for system

function modeling, process modeling, information and data modeling, ontology

modeling and representation, software and system behavioral and structural

design, systems dynamics modeling. The student will also learn the standard

frameworks for structuring the systems development process and documenting the

resulting artifacts. Finally the student will learn the core techniques for system

architecture analysis including: requirements analysis, allocation, assessment, and

design traceability, 2) design verification, validation, and completeness (VV&C), 3)

cost / performance/risk tradeoff analysis, and 4) simulation based (Monte Carlo and

Systems Dynamics) architecture performance assessment. In summary the learning

objectives of this course include:

1.  Mastery of component methods for system Architecting:

a.  Activity Modeling

b.  Process Modeling

c.  Information and Data Modeling

d.  Knowledge Modeling

e.  Factor Trees and Causal Loop Diagrams

f.  Solution Creation with DoDAF AV, CV, OV, DIV-1, SysML

g.  System Design with SysML, DoDAF SV, DIV-2/3

2.  Mastery of “framework” of methods

for structuring the approach to systems development

3.  Familiarization with basic architecting and analysis methods :

a.  Symptom collection and diagnosis

b.  Need condition formulation

c.  Requirement constraint formulation

d.  Requirements Analysis, Allocation, Assessment, and Traceability

e.  Analysis of Alternatives

f.  Verification, Validation, and Completeness (VV&C)

g.  Cost / Performance Based Trades

h.  Simulation based architecture evaluation and performance

assessment

4.  Familiarization with documentation for system development life cycle

artifacts

None

Grading Policy

grading policy

Grading policy

Grading policy

Class attendance is required unless otherwise arranged with the instructor.

Homework is due as stated on each Canvas managed assignment.  Class

time will be used to discuss HW solutions.  Late homework may or may

not be accepted at the sole discretion of the instructor, except as provided

for in the Student Rules regarding Academics at  http://student-

rules.tamu.edu . Take home examinations are due at the time and date stated

on the Canvas assignment. Late examination submissions are not accepted,

except as provided for in the Student Rules regarding Academics

at  http://student-rules.tamu.edu . The material in this course is best learned

through actual “doing”. Systems Engineering is predominantly practiced a

team environment.

Therefore the grades will be determined through student performance on

on the Canvas assignment. Late examination submissions are not accepted,

except as provided for in the Student Rules regarding Academics

at  http://student-rules.tamu.edu . The material in this course is best learned

through actual “doing”. Systems Engineering is predominantly practiced a

team environment.

Therefore the grades will be determined through student performance on

homework assignments and in class participation as well as through

examinations.

1.  Homework (25%)

2.  Class Participation + Quizzes 25%

3.  Take home mid-term exam  25%

4.  Final exam  25%

Optional assignments and test questions may be made available for extra

credit.

Grading scale

Grading Scale

Grading will be on the Standard Letter Grading Scale:

B = 80-89

Late Work Policy

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not

considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).

Course Schedule

This course will start with the study of component systems as simple or complex

hybrids, involving information, computation, mechanical, and human elements. Later

in the course we will study “capabilities” that are formed as collections of

interoperating systems – referred to in the current vernacular as a system of systems,

or SOS. We will study the way engineered orchestration of the interoperability and

combined performance of the collection will result in a desired capability.

Throughout the course we will study methods, languages, and techniques that enable

us to ‘view’ and analyze the as-is system, develop and communicate the to-be system

requirements, and transition these requirements into the design specification of a new

or modified system. The initial modeling methods focus will be the IDEF function,

information, process, data, and ontology modeling methods.. Our study will be

performed within the context of the systems development life cycle and the use of

frameworks for applying the systems engineering discipline. The DoD Architecture

Framework (DoDAF) will be the primary structure studied. Other enterprise

architectural frameworks such as MoDAF, FEAF, and the Zachman AF will be

overviewed. The SysML/UML languages will be covered as they are a key

component in these frameworks and the current basis for MBSE (Model Based

Systems   Engineering). The primary analysis tools covered will be systems dynamics

simulation. The foundational methods of factor trees and causal loop diagrams that

support creation of systems dynamics simulation will be covered.

Additional Course Information

Required lecture reference readings are included in the lecture slides.

Select prior year lectures, some reference materials/required readings, and this

semesters lectures will be made available on Canvas. It is highly recommended that

students avail themselves of this resource.

AI Statement

ISEN 641 is interesting in that the SE methods and frameworks discussed are used

to represent knowledge and would likely fit under the symbolic view of AI (versus

say the connectionist view of AI highlighted by heavy emphasis on data processing

and pattern extraction).

SE Methods produce models that embody the very knowledge that AI technologies

seek to leverage (at least that's the hope through elements such as the RAG).

If you can find AI apps that produce the models, using the methods, that we are

covering in the course then by all means make it part of the class discussion.  If you

have a question regarding the use of AI in this course then please ask.  I worked for

Dr. Richard Mayer at KBSI for 30y (the developer of this course).  It's an AI company

and he will be an observer in the course as well.  A major goal of the course is to

produce models that represent knowledge that can used by AI applications.

The bottom line is this - 641 is about the human use of SE methods and techniques

to produce models that can be used both by humans and AI apps.  Using AI apps to

generate these models and their associated design rationale is not an objective of

the course and any attempt to do so must be first cleared with the instructor.
